g1u06.w.to-live.note: TRUTH/SENSE: d "To have your room and bed in a city." wrongly restricts live (g=wohnen) to a CITY. One lives in a village, the woods, anywhere — the city only appears because the carrier uses York. The definition encodes a false restriction; a student infers live = 'have a home in a city'.

g1u06.w.to-catch.note: TRUTH/SENSE: d "The dog runs and gets the ball in its mouth." defines catch as grabbing a thrown object in the mouth, but the carrier s "Detectives always ___ the bad people." uses catch = apprehend/festnehmen. The definition's scenario does not license the sentence's sense; a child reading the dog-and-ball d cannot derive 'catch the bad people'.

g1u06.w.to-jump.note: TRUTH/SENSE: carrier s "A bird ___ on Sherlock's head." with full answer 'jumps' is an unnatural collocation — birds land or hop onto a head, they do not 'jump on' it. Models a false-to-life use of jump for the student.

g1u06.w.river.note: TRUTH/SENSE: d "This is long and cold and runs in the woods. You can swim in it." presents 'cold' and 'runs in the woods' as defining properties of a river. Rivers are not inherently cold and most do not run in woods; these are narrative props (swimming in summer) misframed as part of the meaning of river (g=Fluss).
